notes: searches: ~18, clusters: 13. Control sample of needs_verification:false claims: GC-12-108, GC-12-133, GC-12-144. Most attributed quotes trace to D'Aubigné 'History of the Reformation' and Wylie 'History of Protestantism' — White's named sources; verified at cluster level. Anachronistic verdicts: GC-12-027 (Marguerite 'accepted reformed faith'), GC-12-035 (Briçonnet 'stake or recant'), GC-12-114 (Louis XVI 'struggled'). Providential long-range causal claims (097, 116) marked unverifiable.

GC-12-001.rationale: [common_knowledge] Protest of Spires (1529) and Confession of Augsburg (1530) are standard markers of the German Reformation, followed by confessional conflict.

GC-12-002.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Schmalkaldic War (1546-47) between Charles V and the Protestant Schmalkaldic League is textbook fact.

GC-12-003.rationale: [common_knowledge] Duke Maurice of Saxony, a Protestant, allied with Charles V against the Schmalkaldic League (1546-47) — the standard 'betrayal' of the Protestant cause.

GC-12-004.rationale: [common_knowledge] John Frederick I of Saxony (captured at Mühlberg 1547) and Philip of Hesse were held captive in Charles V's itinerant court.

GC-12-005.rationale: [common_knowledge] Maurice of Saxony's 1552 revolt forced Charles V into flight, reversing his 1547 triumph.

GC-12-006.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Peace of Passau (1552) and Peace of Augsburg (1555) granted legal toleration to Lutheranism, which Charles V had fought.

GC-12-007.rationale: [common_knowledge] By the mid-1550s Charles V's finances were exhausted and his territories strained while Protestantism continued to expand — standard characterization of his late reign.

GC-12-008.rationale: [common_knowledge] Charles V abdicated (1556) and retired to the monastery of Yuste, worn by gout and struggle at age ~56.

GC-12-009.rationale: Theological framing of Charles V's opposition as suppressing divinely ordained 'light'; not an empirical claim.

GC-12-010.rationale: [common_knowledge] Swiss cantons divided between Protestant (Zurich, Bern) and Catholic (the Forest cantons) — textbook fact.

GC-12-011.rationale: [common_knowledge] Confessional tension between Protestant and Catholic cantons produced the Kappel Wars (1529, 1531). GC's 'persecution of those who desired truth' simplifies mixed political/confessional/economic causes.

GC-12-012.rationale: [common_knowledge] Huldrych Zwingli died at the Second Battle of Kappel, 11 October 1531, along with many Zurich reformers.

GC-12-013.rationale: [common_knowledge] Oecolampadius died 24 November 1531, weeks after Kappel; the 'overcome by disasters' causal framing is traditional (he died of illness shortly after).

GC-12-014.rationale: Theological claim about divine providence not abandoning the Protestant cause.

GC-12-015.rationale: Jacques Lefèvre d'Étaples was an aged, learned professor at the University of Paris and a devout Catholic who became an early French reformist influence.

GC-12-016.rationale: Lefèvre's humanist study of ancient texts led to biblical scholarship (his 1509 Psalter and 1512 Pauline commentary) and he taught Scripture to students.

GC-12-017.rationale: Lefèvre worked on hagiographic/saints' material before turning to Scripture; the detail derives from D'Aubigné's account (White's cited source).

GC-12-018.rationale: Lefèvre's 1512 commentary on the Pauline epistles teaches justification by grace alone through faith ('it is God alone who provides this righteousness through faith and justifies by grace alone unto life eternal'), predating Luther/Zwingli. Nearly verbatim match.

sources.title: Lefèvre Took Risks To Teach Righteousness By Faith — Christian History Institute
Jacques Lefèvre d'Étaples: a humanist or a reformist view of Paul? (academia.edu)

GC-12-019.rationale: Attributed quote from Lefèvre's writings on the atonement ('the exchange'); reproduced in D'Aubigné, White's cited source. Not independently located in the primary text within budget.

GC-12-020.rationale: Attributed quote from Lefèvre on church membership and the divine nature; traces to D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-021.rationale: William Farel was raised in a pious Catholic family and was a zealous Catholic before conversion.

GC-12-022.rationale: Farel's recollection 'I would gnash my teeth like a furious wolf' when hearing anti-papal speech is a well-attested quotation in D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

sources.title: The Life of William Farel — American Presbyterian Church

GC-12-023.rationale: Farel was a disciple of Lefèvre and, before conversion, joined in devotional rounds of Paris churches and shrines — attested in D'Aubigné and Farel biographies.

sources.title: The Life of William Farel — American Presbyterian Church

GC-12-024.rationale: Lefèvre's teaching on grace and the atonement as heard by Farel; the quoted phrases trace to D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-025.rationale: Farel's own conversion account ('like a meek and harmless lamb, having his heart entirely withdrawn from the pope, and given to Jesus Christ') is attested in D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-026.rationale: Guillaume Briçonnet, bishop of Meaux, gathered the evangelical humanists (Lefèvre, Farel, Roussel, etc.) into the 'Circle of Meaux'.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-027.rationale: GC says Marguerite (sister of Francis I) 'accepted the reformed faith'. Modern scholarship holds she remained within the Catholic Church, promoting internal evangelical reform while never formally becoming Protestant. The stronger 'accepted the reformed faith' reading reflects 19th-c Protestant historiography (Wylie/D'Aubigné) that claimed her for the Reformation.
GC-12-027.modern_consensus: Marguerite de Navarre was an evangelical reformist and protector of reformers who remained Catholic and avoided a break with the papacy.
GC-12-027.period_historiography: D'Aubigné and Wylie present the king's sister as embracing the reformed/Protestant faith.

sources.title: King's sister, queen of dissent: Marguerite of Navarre — Medievalists.net
Marguerite de Navarre — World History Encyclopedia
sources.note: 'Marguerite remained within the Catholic Church, pushing for internal reform, while avoiding a full break with the papacy.'

GC-12-028.rationale: Francis I and his mother Louise of Savoy initially tolerated/favored the evangelical humanists (early 1520s) before the policy hardened.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-029.rationale: Briçonnet actively reformed his Meaux diocese, instructing clergy and laity and instituting biblical preaching.

GC-12-030.rationale: Briçonnet's reforms at Meaux included replacing negligent clergy and bringing in learned evangelical preachers.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-031.rationale: Lefèvre's French New Testament appeared in 1523, close in time to Luther's German NT (Sept 1522). Minor imprecision: it was printed in Paris by Simon de Colines, though editions of Gospels/Epistles were made 'for the use of the diocese of Meaux' and Lefèvre was then vicar-general at Meaux.
GC-12-031.modern_consensus: French NT printed Paris 1523 by Colines, intended for the Meaux diocese.

GC-12-032.rationale: Briçonnet promoted circulation of the French New Testament in his parishes; Meaux laypeople gained access to Scripture in the vernacular.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-033.rationale: Descriptive claim about Meaux believers gathering to read Scripture; derives from D'Aubigné's narrative, White's cited source. Not independently documentable in detail.

GC-12-034.rationale: Royal protection restrained persecution at Meaux for a time before the Sorbonne/Parlement prevailed (mid-1520s), consistent with the documented collapse of the Circle of Meaux.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-035.rationale: GC dramatizes Briçonnet as 'forced to choose between the fire and recantation'. Modern scholarship shows he appeared before the Paris Parlement, gave guarantees to the Sorbonne that public Scripture instruction would cease, and was acquitted of heresy in November 1525 (fined 200 livres) — he was never condemned to the stake. The 'stake or recant' framing traces to White's Reformation sources.
GC-12-035.modern_consensus: Briçonnet submitted to Parlement pressure and issued anti-Lutheran synodal measures; acquitted 1525, remained bishop until his death in 1534. No death sentence.
GC-12-035.period_historiography: D'Aubigné/Wylie dramatize the bishop as forced to choose recantation over burning.

sources.title: Reformation & Repression under Bishop Briçonnet of Meaux — World History Encyclopedia
Henry M. Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots (CCEL)
sources.note: Acquitted Nov 1525, fined 200 livres; gave guarantees to the Sorbonne

GC-12-036.rationale: Despite Briçonnet's retreat, Meaux produced martyrs (e.g. the wool-carder Jean Leclerc, and later Meaux believers burned in 1546), so some followers stood firm to death.

sources.title: Henry M. Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots (CCEL)

GC-12-037.rationale: Louis de Berquin was of noble birth (a nobleman of Artois).

GC-12-038.rationale: The description of Berquin as a strict Catholic who abhorred Lutheranism before conversion derives from Theodore Beza/Crespin as relayed by D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-039.rationale: The claim that Berquin found in the Bible 'the doctrines of Luther' derives from D'Aubigné's account of his conversion, White's cited source.

GC-12-040.rationale: The Beza saying that Berquin 'would have been a second Luther had he found in Francis I a second elector' is well attested in the Reformation histories (D'Aubigné, Baird).

sources.title: Henry M. Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots — Berquin (CCEL)

GC-12-041.rationale: The cry 'He is worse than Luther' attributed to Berquin's opponents derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-042.rationale: Berquin was imprisoned as a heretic (first in 1523) and released through Francis I's intervention.

GC-12-043.rationale: Berquin was imprisoned three times by Parlement/Sorbonne (1523, 1525/26, 1529) and freed by the king each of the first times; the third (1529) ended in execution during the king's absence.

sources.title: Louis de Berquin — Christian History Institute

GC-12-044.rationale: Erasmus corresponded with Berquin urging caution and exile, warning him not to trust the king's protection and not to implicate Erasmus with the theology faculty; his description of Béda as a 'thousand-headed monster' is attested.

sources.title: Henry M. Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots — Berquin (CCEL)

GC-12-045.rationale: Berquin drew propositions (reported as twelve) from the theologians' works, declared them heretical/unbiblical, and appealed to the king to judge; attested in D'Aubigné/Baird.

sources.title: Henry M. Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots — Berquin (CCEL)

GC-12-046.rationale: The account that Francis I bade the theologians defend their case by the Bible derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-047.rationale: A statue of the Virgin at a Paris street corner was mutilated (the 1528 defacing of a Virgin image on the rue des Rosiers), which provoked expiatory processions — the episode GC places near Berquin's 1529 trial.

GC-12-048.rationale: The monks' cry blaming Berquin's doctrines and alleging a Lutheran conspiracy against religion, law, and throne derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-049.rationale: Berquin was condemned and executed rapidly (17/22 April 1529) while Francis I was absent, precluding royal intervention.

sources.title: Louis de Berquin — Christian History Institute

GC-12-050.rationale: The detail that Berquin was led to execution at noon derives from D'Aubigné's eyewitness-based account, White's cited source.

GC-12-051.rationale: The description of Berquin's clothing at execution ('a cloak of velvet, a doublet of satin and damask, and golden hose') is directly from D'Aubigné's History of the Reformation, White's cited source.

sources.title: D'Aubigné, History of the Reformation in Europe in the Time of Calvin

GC-12-052.rationale: The onlookers' remark that Berquin looked like one meditating in a temple derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-053.rationale: D'Aubigné records that at the stake Berquin tried to speak and monks shouted and soldiers clashed their arms to drown his voice — reproduced by White.

GC-12-054.rationale: Berquin was executed in 1529; the quoted line about setting 'the populace of 1793 the base example' is Wylie's (History of Protestantism), White's cited source. Date 1529 correct.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-055.rationale: In consideration of his nobility, Berquin was strangled before his body was burned.

GC-12-056.rationale: The witnesses' declaration of readiness to meet death cheerfully derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

GC-12-057.rationale: After Meaux, Lefèvre took refuge (1525) in Strasbourg, a German-speaking free imperial city; GC's 'made his way to Germany' fits loosely.

GC-12-058.rationale: Farel returned to his native Gap (Dauphiné) to preach. Gap lies in south-eastern France; GC's 'eastern France' is a loose geographic characterization.

GC-12-059.rationale: Farel's preaching provoked the authorities and he was driven out — consistent with his repeated expulsions in this period.

GC-12-060.rationale: Farel's statement about crosses and persecutions being severer than he could bear alone derives from his correspondence via D'Aubigné/Herminjard, White's cited source.

GC-12-061.rationale: [common_knowledge] Acts 8:4 (KJV): 'they that were scattered abroad went every where preaching the word' — accurately quoted and applied typologically.

GC-12-062.rationale: Young Calvin was a brilliant, devout student in Paris expected to become a distinguished churchman.

GC-12-063.rationale: Pierre Robert Olivétan, Calvin's relative (cousin), was an early evangelical; the tradition that he influenced Calvin is standard, though its precise role is traditional.

GC-12-064.rationale: Olivétan's 'two religions' speech derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source; not independently documented as a verbatim utterance.

GC-12-065.rationale: Calvin's rejoinder rejecting 'new doctrines' derives from D'Aubigné's narrative, White's cited source.

GC-12-066.rationale: Interior/spiritual claim about Calvin's failed penances during his conversion crisis; derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source. Not externally verifiable.

GC-12-067.rationale: The scene of Calvin witnessing a heretic's burning in a Paris square is a D'Aubigné-derived narrative detail, White's cited source; not independently documented.

GC-12-068.rationale: Calvin's atonement prayer derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources; not independently located in Calvin's works.

GC-12-069.rationale: Calvin received the tonsure and the chaplaincy of the altar of La Gésine in Noyon cathedral in May 1521, at age twelve — confirmed by standard biography.

sources.title: Calvin's Youth and Training — Schaff, History of the Christian Church

GC-12-070.rationale: Calvin held the benefice/title and drew its income without ordination or priestly duties, the actual duties performed by others — standard account of clerical benefices held by minors.

GC-12-071.rationale: Calvin turned from a clerical track to legal studies (Orléans, Bourges) at his father's direction, then devoted himself to the evangelical cause after his conversion.

GC-12-072.rationale: Calvin's remark on being 'exalted to so great a dignity' (referring to gospel ministry, not social status) derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-073.rationale: After leaving Paris (post-Cop affair, 1533-34) Calvin took refuge in territories under Marguerite's protection (Angoulême, Nérac), where she sheltered evangelicals.

sources.title: Calvin as a Wandering Evangelist 1533-1536 — Schaff

GC-12-074.rationale: Calvin returned to Paris after a period away, consistent with his itinerant 1533-34 movements.

sources.title: Calvin as a Wandering Evangelist — Schaff

GC-12-075.rationale: During Francis I's absence Marguerite sponsored evangelical preaching in Paris (Gérard Roussel's 1533 Lenten sermons), attested history.

sources.title: Marguerite de Navarre — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-076.rationale: When Roussel's church preaching was opposed, it moved to the royal palace (the Louvre) with large audiences, consistent with the 1533 episode under Marguerite's patronage.

sources.title: Marguerite de Navarre — World History Encyclopedia

GC-12-077.rationale: The detail that Francis I ordered two Paris churches opened for the preaching derives from D'Aubigné, White's cited source.

GC-12-078.rationale: The 'two years' of preaching in Paris with majority rejection derives from D'Aubigné's narrative, White's cited source; the precise duration is not firmly documented (the intense evangelical preaching centered on 1533).

GC-12-079.rationale: Francis I's toleration was politically calculated and short-lived; the Catholic party regained ascendancy and persecution resumed (culminating after the 1534 Placards).

GC-12-080.rationale: After the Cop affair (Nov 1533) Calvin came under suspicion and his rooms were searched; the 'determined to bring him to the flames' is the dramatized D'Aubigné framing.

GC-12-081.rationale: In the Cop affair, Calvin was warned that officers were coming and fled his lodging.

GC-12-082.rationale: Traditional accounts have Calvin escaping Paris (Cop affair) lowered from a window by knotted sheets and fleeing disguised as a vinedresser with a hoe. GC reproduces the hoe-disguise version of this standard (if somewhat legendary) account.

sources.title: Why Did Calvin Slide Down a Rope and Dress as a Farmer? — Christian History Institute

GC-12-083.rationale: Calvin fled south and again took refuge in Marguerite's domains (Angoulême/Nérac).

sources.title: Calvin as a Wandering Evangelist — Schaff

GC-12-084.rationale: Calvin went to Poitiers (1534), a university town where evangelical opinions had followers.

sources.title: Calvin as a Wandering Evangelist — Schaff

GC-12-085.rationale: Per an 'uncertain tradition' (Schaff), Calvin celebrated the Lord's Supper in the Reformed manner for the first time in a cave near Poitiers ('Calvin's Cave'). The claim it was the first such celebration in France is traditional and not firmly established.
GC-12-085.modern_consensus: Traditional 'Calvin's Cave' communion near Poitiers; historians flag it as an uncertain tradition.

sources.title: Calvin as a Wandering Evangelist — Schaff

GC-12-086.rationale: Calvin decided to leave France for German-speaking lands (Basel, then Strasbourg) around 1534-35.

GC-12-087.rationale: The Affair of the Placards (night of 17-18 October 1534) posted anti-Mass placards in Paris and several provincial cities (Blois, Rouen, Tours, Orléans, Amboise).

GC-12-088.rationale: The Placards ended Francis I's conciliatory policy and gave Catholic authorities a pretext to demand harsh repression of Protestants as a threat to the throne and order.

GC-12-089.rationale: One placard was posted on the door of Francis I's bedchamber at Amboise, a notorious breach that personally shook the king.

sources.title: The Affair of the Placards that rocked Amboise — Château d'Amboise

GC-12-090.rationale: Francis I's furious order to seize and exterminate all suspected of 'Lutheresy' derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources; it captures the documented hardening of royal policy after the Placards.

GC-12-091.rationale: The story of a coerced Protestant artisan-informer leading the search derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-092.rationale: Jean Morin, lieutenant-criminel of the Châtelet, led the post-Placards persecution in Paris; described in the Reformation histories White cites.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-093.rationale: The 'Morin made all the city quake / reign of terror' passage is Wylie's (History of Protestantism), White's cited source, describing the house-to-house searches of the post-Placards crackdown.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-094.rationale: Post-Placards executions in Paris used the estrapade, in which victims were repeatedly lowered into and raised from the fire to prolong agony — documented cruelty of the crackdown.

GC-12-095.rationale: The account of scaffolds distributed across Paris quarters with staggered burnings to spread terror is Wylie's, White's cited source, consistent with the documented public spectacle of the executions.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-096.rationale: Post-Placards propaganda charged Protestants with plotting massacre, sedition, and regicide, justifying repression as defense of throne and order.

GC-12-097.rationale: Providential retribution claim: that 1530s persecution of Protestants 'accumulated in a weight of retribution' producing the later doom of monarchy and church. This is a theological interpretation of history, not an empirically testable causal assertion; modern historiography does not treat the Revolution as retribution for 16th-c persecution.
GC-12-097.modern_consensus: Historians do not endorse a direct providential causal link between 16th-c persecution of French Protestants and the 1789-93 Revolution.

GC-12-098.rationale: The post-Placards crackdown caused an exodus of scholars, printers, and others from Paris (notable exiles include Calvin, Clément Marot).

GC-12-099.rationale: [common_knowledge] Francis I was a leading Renaissance patron of learning who gathered scholars (and founded the lecteurs royaux, later the Collège de France).

GC-12-100.rationale: On 13 January 1535 Francis I issued an edict suppressing all printing in France under penalty of hanging; it was soon modified rather than enforced as an absolute ban.

sources.title: Francis I Bans the Publishing of Books, 1535 — Landmark Events

GC-12-101.rationale: Clergy demanded blood-expiation for the affront of the Placards to the Mass and public royal sanction of the repression; Francis publicly led the January 1535 expiatory procession.

sources.title: 1535: Six Protestants for the Affair of the Placards — Executed Today

GC-12-102.rationale: A grand expiatory procession was held in Paris on 21 January 1535.

sources.title: 1535: Six Protestants for the Affair of the Placards — Executed Today
January 21, 1535: French Protestants Burned in Front of Notre Dame — History and Headlines

GC-12-103.rationale: The procession's staging (mourning drapery on houses, altars at intervals) is Wylie's descriptive detail, White's cited source, consistent with the elaborate documented ritual.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-104.rationale: The procession carried the host under canopy with the highest civil and ecclesiastical dignitaries; the king walked after the sacrament without crown or robes of state — documented as an unprecedentedly solemn procession.

GC-12-105.rationale: Francis I marched bareheaded, eyes down, carrying a lighted penitential taper, in the character of a penitent.

GC-12-106.rationale: Francis I's speech vowing to cut off even his own infected limb or sacrifice his own child rather than tolerate heresy is attested in the Reformation histories (D'Aubigné/Wylie); the king gave a fervent anti-heresy oration that day.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-107.rationale: The assembly's weeping and cry 'We will live and die for the Catholic religion' derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-108.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Theological/moralizing framing that France, rejecting the light, fell into darkness. No smuggled empirical assertion; extractor's non-flag is appropriate.

GC-12-109.rationale: The 1535 expiatory procession culminated at Notre-Dame; roughly three centuries later (1793) the same cathedral hosted the revolutionary Festival/Cult of Reason. Both facts hold; 1535->1793 is 258 years.

sources.title: Notre-Dame de Paris — Wikipedia (Cult of Reason 1793)

GC-12-110.rationale: Six Protestants were burned in Paris in conjunction with the 21 January 1535 procession; the staging near the route, timed to the day's ceremonial, is attested.

sources.title: 1535: Six Protestants for the Affair of the Placards — Executed Today

GC-12-111.rationale: The martyr's confession of faith in the prophets and apostles derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-112.rationale: The closing scene of king and prelates withdrawing satisfied and resolved to continue the repression derives from Wylie's narrative, White's cited source, and matches the documented intensification of persecution.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 13

GC-12-113.rationale: Louis XVI was executed on 21 January 1793 in Paris; 1793 minus 1535 = 258 years, and both events fell on 21 January. The date arithmetic and coincidence are accurate.

GC-12-114.rationale: GC (following Wylie) depicts Louis XVI 'struggling hand to hand with his jailers and executioners... dragged forward... held down by main force'. The dominant modern account has Louis go to the scaffold with composure, address the crowd (drums drowning him), and submit; his only resistance was a brief objection to having his hands bound, which he accepted on his confessor's counsel. The violent-struggle version reflects 19th-c dramatized historiography.
GC-12-114.modern_consensus: Louis XVI met his execution with dignity; he addressed the crowd and submitted, after briefly resisting the binding of his hands.
GC-12-114.period_historiography: Wylie's account portrays the king as physically forced to the block.

sources.title: On This Day in 1793, Revolutionaries Executed the King — Smithsonian Magazine

GC-12-115.rationale: About 2,600-2,800 people were guillotined in Paris during the Reign of Terror (modern estimate ~2,625). GC's figure of 2,800 is in the right range; 'near the same spot' loosely fits the Place de la Révolution, though executions also occurred elsewhere in Paris.
GC-12-115.modern_consensus: ~2,625 executed by guillotine in Paris during the Terror.

sources.title: The French Revolution executed royals and nobles... — The Conversation

GC-12-116.rationale: Providential causal thesis: that France's 16th-c rejection of Protestantism 'inevitably' produced the Revolution and Reign of Terror. This is a theological reading of history, not an empirically testable causal claim; modern historiography does not endorse the inevitability or the direct causal chain.
GC-12-116.modern_consensus: Historians attribute the Revolution to Enlightenment ideas plus fiscal, social, and political crises, not to divine retribution for Reformation-era persecution.

GC-12-117.rationale: Farel fled France and worked in French-speaking Switzerland from the mid-1520s, advancing the Reformation alongside Zwingli's Swiss work (Zwingli d. 1531).

GC-12-118.rationale: French exiles (notably at Neuchâtel) translated and printed Reformation works and French Bibles, distributed into France by colporteurs — well-documented Reformation book trade.

GC-12-119.rationale: Farel began his Swiss work under the humble guise of a schoolmaster (e.g. at Aigle, using a pseudonym).

GC-12-120.rationale: The priests' argument that a gospel bringing war cannot be Christ's derives from D'Aubigné/Wylie, White's cited sources.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

GC-12-121.rationale: Farel was repeatedly assaulted by hostile crowds and beaten during his Swiss preaching campaigns.

GC-12-122.rationale: Farel's preaching led to the Reformation and removal of images in Neuchâtel (iconoclasm of 23 October 1530); Morat (Murten) likewise adopted the Reformation around this period.

sources.title: 23 octobre 1530 - Neuchâtel se rallie à la Réforme — Herodote.net

GC-12-123.rationale: Farel long sought to bring the Reformation to Geneva, which he entered in the early 1530s.

GC-12-124.rationale: Farel first entered Geneva (1532) with a companion and met immediate clerical opposition after brief preaching.

GC-12-125.rationale: Farel was summoned before a Geneva ecclesiastical council in 1532 amid clerical hostility that nearly cost his life; the detail of concealed weapons is D'Aubigné/Wylie dramatization of a genuinely dangerous confrontation.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

GC-12-126.rationale: The armed mob outside the council is a Wylie/D'Aubigné detail, White's cited source, dramatizing the real threat to Farel in Geneva 1532.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

GC-12-127.rationale: Farel and his companion were escorted to safety and left Geneva after the 1532 confrontation.

sources.title: Wylie, History of Protestantism, Vol. 2 Bk 14

GC-12-128.rationale: Antoine Froment, a young evangelist of humble bearing, was sent to Geneva after Farel's first attempt.

GC-12-129.rationale: Froment went to Geneva (Nov 1532) and opened a school, using it as a platform for evangelical preaching.

GC-12-130.rationale: After Froment's groundwork, the Protestant preachers returned (backed by Bern) and Protestant worship was ultimately established in Geneva (official 1535/36).

GC-12-131.rationale: Geneva officially adopted the Reformation (May 1536) before Calvin arrived there (July/August 1536).

GC-12-132.rationale: In 1536 war between Francis I and Charles V blocked Calvin's direct route, forcing him to detour through Geneva on his way to Basel/Strasbourg.

sources.title: John Calvin: Arrival in Geneva — Christian Study Library

GC-12-133.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Largely interpretive/moralizing, but it embeds a checkable fact: Geneva had cast off papal authority (true, 1535/36). The claim that the populace retained old vices matches Calvin's own documented complaints, though the moral judgment is not independently quantifiable. Mild extractor blind spot noted; embedded fact is supported.

GC-12-134.rationale: Calvin's own testimony that he felt 'as if God had from heaven laid his mighty hand upon me to arrest me' when Farel adjured him to stay in Geneva is well attested.

sources.title: John Calvin: Arrival in Geneva — Christian Study Library

GC-12-135.rationale: Reformation Geneva faced papal condemnation and external military threat (notably from the Duke of Savoy) in this period.

GC-12-136.rationale: The Society of Jesus was founded and papally approved in 1540 (bull Regimini militantis Ecclesiae, 27 Sept 1540). GC's date is correct; 'most cruel, unscrupulous' is polemical characterization, not a factual claim.

GC-12-137.rationale: The Jesuits became a major Counter-Reformation educational force, running colleges for elites and schools for common people, drawing pupils (including from Protestant families) into Catholic practice.

GC-12-138.rationale: Jesuits did gain influence at Catholic courts (royal confessors, advisors). GC's 'under various disguises... shaping the policy of nations' overlays anti-Jesuit polemic on a factual core.

GC-12-139.rationale: Pope Paul III's bull Licet ab initio (21 July 1542) established the Roman Inquisition (Congregation of the Holy Office). GC's linkage of this specifically to empowering the Jesuits is White's framing; the bull was Cardinal Carafa's initiative, not a Jesuit instrument.
GC-12-139.modern_consensus: Roman Inquisition established 1542 by Licet ab initio; distinct from the Jesuit order.

GC-12-140.rationale: Counter-Reformation persecution killed or exiled large numbers of Protestants across multiple countries, including many educated and skilled people.

GC-12-141.rationale: [common_knowledge] The Dutch Revolt against Spanish rule (Spain then Europe's greatest power) is textbook fact and advanced the Protestant cause in the Netherlands.

GC-12-142.rationale: [common_knowledge] Sweden adopted Lutheranism under the Vasa monarchy and later, under Gustavus Adolphus, decisively aided the Protestant cause in the Thirty Years' War.

GC-12-143.rationale: Calvin worked at Geneva from 1536 (with a 1538-1541 exile) until his death in 1564 — spanning nearly thirty years, first building the Genevan church, then promoting the Reformation across Europe.

GC-12-144.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Evaluative/interpretive judgment that Calvin's leadership was not faultless and his doctrines not error-free. This is a value judgment, not an empirically testable proposition; extractor's non-flag is appropriate.

GC-12-145.rationale: [common_knowledge] Calvin's Geneva became a refuge and training center for persecuted Protestants from across Western Europe.

GC-12-146.rationale: John Knox and Marian-exile English Protestants, together with Dutch, Spanish, and French Huguenot refugees, sojourned in Geneva and carried Reformed influence home. 'Puritans' is a mildly anachronistic label for the mid-century English exiles.
